title: Bridge Essay: [Distinguishing the voice of self from others: the self-monitoring hypothesis of auditory hallucination].
concept: Voice-hearing as spectrum phenomenon — auditory verbal hallucinations, agency detection, inner speech.

body.text: This bridge paper connects the esoteric/philosophical concept of **Daimon** with mechanistic science from PubMed/MEDLINE.
**Bridge rationale:** Daimonic voice — voice-hearing continuum, inner speech misattribution, corollary discharge.
**Phase context:** Voice-hearing as spectrum phenomenon — auditory verbal hallucinations, agency detection, inner speech.

**[Distinguishing the voice of self from others: the self-monitoring hypothesis of auditory hallucination].** — Shinrigaku Kenkyu (2010). DOI: 10.4992/jjpsy.81.247. PMID: 20845731. Auditory hallucinations (AH), a psychopathological phenomenon where a person hears non-existent voices, commonly occur in schizophrenia. Recent cognitive and neuroscience studies suggest that AH may be the misattribution of one's own inner speech. Self-monitoring through neural feedback mechanisms allows individuals to distinguish between their own and others' actions, including speech. AH maybe the results of an individual's inability to discriminate between their own speech and that of others.

notes: Auto-acquired from PubMed/MEDLINE on 2026-07-12. Phase 13 (Daimon). Evaluation: Good (score=2.8)
